title: About Me
text:
Statistics (Data Science) @ BYU · CS & Math Minor
Email: bnandin1@byu.edu · GitHub: nandinnnnnnnnnnnn · LinkedIn: Nandintsetseg Batsaikhan 



My name is Nandintsetseg Batsaikhan, and I am an undergraduate student at Brigham Young University majoring in Statistics with a Data Science emphasis, with minors in Computer Science and Mathematics. I am interested in data analysis, statistical modeling, and building data-driven applications that solve real-world problems.
My academic work has focused on probability, statistical inference, SQL databases, and programming in Python and R. My long-term goal is to work in a data-focused role where I can combine statistics, programming, and critical thinking to support decision-making.




I’m from Mongolia. Outside of school, I enjoy skiing, hiking, and watching movies.

title: Blog
text: Diagram comparing looping vs vectorized operations in Pandas and NumPy

title: Blog
text:
Problem Statement
In statistical computing, performance matters. When working with large datasets in Pandas and NumPy, using loops can make your code extremely slow. This becomes a serious problem when analyzing real-world data with thousands or millions of rows.
Vectorization is a powerful concept that allows operations to run much faster by applying computations to entire arrays at once instead of processing one element at a time.
In this blog, I will explain the difference between loops and vectorization, demonstrate both approaches, compare their speed, and share my personal experience learning this concept in my statistics and data science classes. Vectorization is a core feature of NumPy. See the official NumPy documentation on vectorization for more examples.

title: Blog
section: My Personal Experience: When My Code Was Too Slow
text:
My Personal Experience: When My Code Was Too Slow
During my STAT 180 and STAT 386 classes, I worked with datasets like cereal nutrition data, weather data, titanic and iris datasets. At first, I wrote my code using loops because it felt natural. I was thinking step-by-step, like solving math manually.
For example, when calculating a new column based on two existing columns, I wrote code like this:
# slow loop version
for i in range(len(df)):
    df["new"][i] = df["a"][i] + df["b"][i]
It worked. But when I ran it on larger datasets, it became very slow. Sometimes my notebook would freeze, or my Streamlit app would take too long to load. Later, I learned vectorization. This changed everything.

title: Blog
text:
Understanding Loops vs Vectorization

Loop Implementation (Slow)
A loop processes data one row at a time. Example:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "a": [1, 2, 3, 4, 5],
    "b": [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
})

# slow method
df["new"] = 0

for i in range(len(df)):
    df["new"][i] = df["a"][i] + df["b"][i]

print(df)
What happens here:

Python reads each row individually
It performs the addition
It stores the result
It repeats this process many times

This is inefficient because Python loops are slow.

title: Blog
text:
Vectorized Implementation (Fast)
Vectorization performs the operation on the entire column at once.
# fast vectorized method
df["new"] = df["a"] + df["b"]

print(df)
What happens here:

Pandas uses optimized internal C code
It processes all values at once
No Python loop is needed

This is dramatically faster.

title: Blog
text:
Why Vectorization Is Faster
Vectorization is faster because:

It avoids Python loops
It uses optimized low-level C implementations
It operates on entire arrays instead of individual elements
It reduces overhead

This is especially important in statistical computing where datasets are large.

title: Blog
text:
Example Using NumPy
NumPy is built entirely around vectorization.

Loop version:
import numpy as np

a = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
b = np.array([10, 20, 30, 40, 50])
result = np.zeros(len(a))

for i in range(len(a)):
    result[i] = a[i] + b[i]


Vectorized version:
result = a + b
This second version is faster and simpler.

title: Blog
text:
Speed Comparison
Here is a real performance comparison:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import time

n = 1000000

df = pd.DataFrame({
    "a": np.random.rand(n),
    "b": np.random.rand(n)
})
# loop timing
start = time.time()
df["new"] = 0
for i in range(len(df)):
    df["new"][i] = df["a"][i] + df["b"][i]

loop_time = time.time() - start

# vectorized timing
start = time.time()

df["new"] = df["a"] + df["b"]
vector_time = time.time() - start

print("Loop time:", loop_time)
print("Vectorized time:", vector_time)
Typical result:
Loop time: 8.5 seconds
Vectorized time: 0.03 seconds

title: Blog
section: My Real Experience in Data Science Projects
text:
My Real Experience in Data Science Projects
When I was building my Streamlit data science app and analyzing iris datasets, I initially used loops for calculations like:

computing differences
normalizing values
creating new columns

My app was slow and inefficient.
After switching to vectorization, everything became faster instantly.
My plots loaded faster My app responded faster My code became cleaner
This was one of the most important lessons I learned in statistical computing.

title: Blog
section: Why This Matters in Statistical Computing
text:
Why This Matters in Statistical Computing
Statistical computing often involves:

large datasets
repeated calculations
matrix operations
simulations

Vectorization makes these tasks feasible.
Without vectorization, modern data science would be too slow.
Libraries like:

Pandas
NumPy
SciPy
scikit-learn all rely heavily on vectorization.

title: Blog
text:
Summary: Loops vs Vectorization



Method
Speed
Readability
Recommended




Loops
Slow
Harder
No


Vectorization
Fast
Cleaner
Yes

title: Blog
text:
Key Takeaways
Vectorization is one of the most important concepts in statistical computing.
It makes the code:

Faster
Cleaner
More efficient

Always prefer vectorized operations in Pandas and NumPy.
Instead of writing:
for i in range(len(df)):
    df["new"][i] = df["a"][i] + df["b"][i]
Write:

df["new"] = df["a"] + df["b"]
This simple change can make the code over 100x faster.

title: Blog
text:
Call to Action
If you are learning Pandas or NumPy, practice rewriting your loop-based code into vectorized form.
This skill will make you a better data scientist and allow you to work efficiently with large datasets.

title: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API
text:
Music streaming platforms generate massive amounts of data about songs, artists, and listening behavior. Spotify alone hosts tens of millions of tracks, each with metadata such as artist, album, release date, and track length. For students learning data science, APIs provide a powerful way to collect this type of real-world data and transform it into a usable dataset.
In this project, I used the Spotify Web API to build an original dataset of Spotify tracks. Instead of downloading an existing dataset, I collected the data myself using Python and the Spotify search endpoint. The result is a curated dataset containing hundreds of tracks gathered from multiple search queries across different genres and release years.
This project demonstrates how APIs can be used to create a dataset from scratch and how raw API data can be cleaned and transformed into a structured dataset suitable for analysis.
crumbs: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API

title: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API
text:
Music streaming platforms generate massive amounts of data about songs, artists, and listening behavior. Spotify alone hosts tens of millions of tracks, each with metadata such as artist, album, release date, and track length. For students learning data science, APIs provide a powerful way to collect this type of real-world data and transform it into a usable dataset.
In this project, I used the Spotify Web API to build an original dataset of Spotify tracks. Instead of downloading an existing dataset, I collected the data myself using Python and the Spotify search endpoint. The result is a curated dataset containing hundreds of tracks gathered from multiple search queries across different genres and release years.
This project demonstrates how APIs can be used to create a dataset from scratch and how raw API data can be cleaned and transformed into a structured dataset suitable for analysis.
crumbs: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API

title: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API
text:
Motivating Question
When collecting data using the Spotify search API, the results depend heavily on the search query used. Queries like genre:pop, genre:rock, or year:2024 can return very different sets of tracks.
This motivated the central question of this project:
How do characteristics of Spotify tracks—such as duration, release year, and explicit content—vary across different search categories?
Understanding these differences can help illustrate how search queries influence the types of songs returned by the Spotify API.
crumbs: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API

title: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API
text:
Ethical Data Collection
Before collecting the data, it was important to ensure that using the Spotify API was allowed and done responsibly.
The dataset was gathered using the official Spotify Web API, which is designed specifically for developers to access Spotify data programmatically. Spotify provides public documentation and authentication methods for accessing their endpoints.
To follow good data collection practices:

I used the official API authentication method through a Spotify developer application.
No private user data was collected.
Requests were spaced with small delays to avoid excessive API calls.
API credentials were stored in a .env file and were not uploaded to GitHub.

More information about the API can be found in the Spotify developer documentation:
Spotify Web API Documentation
crumbs: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API

title: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API
text:
How the Data Was Collected
The dataset was collected using Python and the Spotipy library, which provides a convenient interface for interacting with the Spotify API.
The general steps were:

Create a Spotify developer application

Obtain a SPOTIFY_CLIENT_ID and SPOTIFY_CLIENT_SECRET.

Authenticate using the Spotipy library

Credentials are loaded securely from a .env file.

Use the Spotify search endpoint

Multiple search queries were used to diversify the dataset.

Collect multiple pages of results

The Spotify API returns results in pages, so offsets were used to retrieve additional tracks.

Store the results in a structured dataset

Track information was saved to a CSV file.


The main search queries used included:

year:2024
year:2023
year:2022
genre:pop
genre:rock
genre:jazz
genre:hip-hop

Using multiple queries allowed the dataset to include a wider range of tracks rather than relying on a single search category.
The raw dataset was saved to: data/raw/spotify_tracks.csv
crumbs: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API

title: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API
text:
Data Cleaning and Transformations
The raw API data required several transformations before it could be used as a clean dataset.
The following steps were applied:

Duplicate tracks were removed using the track_id.
Release year was extracted from the release_date field.
Track duration was converted from milliseconds to minutes.
Explicit content was converted to a numeric indicator (0 or 1).
Rows with missing essential metadata were removed.

The cleaned dataset was saved to: data/processed/spotify_clean.csv
These transformations helped standardize the dataset and create additional useful variables.
crumbs: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API

title: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API
text:
Final Dataset Overview
The final dataset contains over 500 Spotify tracks gathered through the Spotify API.

Dataset Size

Observations: ~500 tracks

Variables: 9–10 features



Data Dictionary



Variable
Description




track_id
Unique Spotify identifier


track_name
Name of the track


artist
Artist name(s)


album
Album the track belongs to


release_date
Original release date


duration_ms
Track length in milliseconds


explicit
Whether the track is marked explicit (1 = yes, 0 = no)


search_query
Query used to retrieve the track


release_year
Year extracted from release date


duration_min
Track duration converted to minutes



The dataset includes several numeric variables, including track duration and release year, which allow for further exploration and analysis.
crumbs: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API

title: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API
text:
Data Quality Considerations
Like many datasets collected through APIs, this dataset has some limitations.
First, the dataset is not a random sample of all Spotify songs. Instead, it reflects the tracks returned by the Spotify search system for the chosen queries.
Second, search results may be influenced by Spotify’s internal ranking algorithms. This means the tracks returned may not represent the full distribution of songs in a given genre or year.
Third, genre-based searches can sometimes return unexpected or loosely related results because Spotify’s search engine does not strictly enforce genre categories.
Despite these limitations, the dataset still provides a useful example of how API-based data collection works and how search queries can shape the resulting dataset.
crumbs: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API

title: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API
text:
Project Repository
The full project code used to collect and clean the dataset is available on GitHub:
GitHub Repository:
[Insert your GitHub repo link here]
The repository contains:

Python scripts for collecting Spotify data
Data cleaning scripts
The curated dataset
Documentation explaining how to reproduce the dataset
crumbs: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API

title: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API
text:
Conclusion
This project demonstrates how APIs can be used to build a dataset from scratch. By using the Spotify Web API, I was able to gather hundreds of tracks and organize them into a structured dataset suitable for analysis.
The project also highlights an important lesson in data science: the way data is collected can strongly influence the patterns we observe. Because the dataset was built using search queries, the resulting tracks reflect how Spotify’s search system categorizes and ranks songs.
For students interested in working with real-world data, APIs provide an excellent opportunity to move beyond prebuilt datasets and create their own.
Future work could extend this project by collecting additional Spotify features such as audio characteristics (danceability, energy, tempo) or by comparing playlists, artists, or listening trends across genres.
Overall, this project provides a practical introduction to API-based data collection, dataset curation, and data cleaning using Python.
crumbs: Collecting Spotify Track Data with the Spotify API
